company.name: ServiceNow
company.description: ServiceNow provides cloud-based workflow automation and enterprise service management software through its Now Platform.

overview.title: Enterprise workflow platform
paragraphs: ServiceNow sells the Now Platform, a cloud software layer used to digitize, automate, and connect enterprise workflows across IT, employee, customer, security, finance, and procurement functions.
The company remains most associated with IT service management, but its broader strategy is to make ServiceNow the shared workflow and AI platform that sits across fragmented enterprise systems.

overview.title: Subscription economics
paragraphs: ServiceNow's 2025 financials show a high-recurring-revenue software model, with annual revenue of about $13.3 billion and GAAP net income of about $1.7 billion.

Its valuation still depends heavily on durable growth, high retention, enterprise platform expansion, and the ability of AI features such as Now Assist to deepen rather than compress workflow software demand.

moatNarrative: ServiceNow's moat is strongest where it becomes a workflow system of record: ticket histories, service catalogs, approvals, integrations, access controls, CMDB data, and custom process logic all create switching costs once embedded across a large enterprise.

The platform also benefits from enterprise procurement trust, partner implementation ecosystems, compliance posture, and cross-sell into adjacent workflows. The risk is that low-code workflow automation and AI-assisted internal tooling make some departments less willing to accept expensive platform sprawl.
decentralizationNarrative: The core ServiceNow model is centralized SaaS: customers rent a managed platform and coordinate workflows inside a vendor-controlled cloud and application ecosystem.

Decentralization pressure is plausible but uneven. Open-source ITSM, workflow engines, and federated workflow registries can replace pieces of the stack, especially for public-sector, cooperative, local-first, or cost-sensitive operators, but large enterprises still value ServiceNow's integrated governance, support, and implementation network.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 101-125; refreshed with public company, product, market-data, and open-source replacement sources reviewed on 2026-05-27.

moat.rationale: ServiceNow has high switching costs from enterprise workflow data, integrations, process customization, governance, partner services, and cross-department expansion.

decentralizability.rationale: Workflow automation and ITSM are software domains with credible open-source replacements, but regulated enterprise customers often prefer centralized SaaS operations, vendor support, and integrated governance.

profitability.rationale: The 2025 annual report showed roughly $13.3 billion in revenue and about $1.7 billion in GAAP net income, with a subscription-heavy software model.

peRatio.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported ServiceNow's trailing P/E ratio at about 58.8 for May 2026; market-data sources vary day to day.

marketCap.rationale: YCharts reported ServiceNow market capitalization of about $103.09 billion for May 26, 2026.

products.name: Now Platform
products.summary: The Now Platform is ServiceNow's cloud platform for building, automating, integrating, and operating enterprise workflows across business functions.
products.whyItMatters: It is the strategic layer that lets ServiceNow expand from IT service management into broader enterprise process automation, AI agents, governance, and cross-department workflow data.
replacementSketch: A plausible replacement stack would combine open workflow engines, open integration tools, open identity and policy controls, and self-hosted process applications under an organization's own governance.
The hard part is not drawing workflow diagrams; it is matching ServiceNow's operational maturity, auditability, permissions, integrations, and enterprise support expectations across many departments.

alternatives.name: Operaton
alternatives.summary: Operaton is an open-source BPMN process automation engine forked from the Camunda 7 lineage.

openness.rationale: Operaton explicitly positions itself as a free and open-source BPMN engine with public source development.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Self-hosted BPMN engines reduce vendor lock-in for workflow execution, though they do not by themselves create federated multi-operator governance.

readiness.rationale: The project builds on a mature BPMN lineage, but a full ServiceNow replacement still requires surrounding applications, integrations, identity, monitoring, and support.

costLeverage.rationale: Open-source process execution can lower licensing leverage for organizations able to operate and extend their own workflow stack.

alternatives.name: n8n
alternatives.summary: n8n is a source-available workflow automation platform for visual automations, custom code, integrations, and self-hosted or cloud deployments.

openness.rationale: The source is visible and self-hostable, but the project is source-available rather than fully open-source under a standard permissive or copyleft license.

decentralizationFit.rationale: n8n can be self-hosted and extended, but its normal usage is still organization-local automation rather than a federated protocol.

readiness.rationale: n8n has broad integration coverage and active adoption, though security and governance hardening matter for enterprise workflow use.

costLeverage.rationale: Self-hosted automation can avoid some SaaS workflow costs, but enterprise-grade operations and governance still carry real implementation expense.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated Workflow Commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated workflow commons would let organizations publish, reuse, and audit workflow templates, integration adapters, process definitions, and policy modules across interoperable servers instead of depending on one SaaS platform's marketplace and runtime.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: ServiceNow's platform power weakens if reusable enterprise workflow assets move into portable, auditable, organization-hosted registries with interoperable execution engines.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federation and portable governance rather than through money. Participating organizations could host their own workflow registries, mirror trusted packages, and choose execution engines while preserving shared schemas and audit trails.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Public agencies, enterprises, vendors, and systems integrators coordinate through signed workflow packages, common BPMN or workflow schemas, compatibility tests, and federated registries of approved process modules.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Packages are signed by maintainers, checked against reproducible test fixtures, scanned for secrets and risky calls, and deployed only after local policy approval. Federated reputation and audit logs help identify maintainers with reliable histories.
failureModes: Workflow portability may break on proprietary integrations, identity models, and undocumented enterprise data assumptions.
A federated registry can become fragmented or unsafe if signing, dependency review, and policy enforcement are weak.
adoptionPath: Start with low-risk shared workflows such as onboarding, procurement requests, access approvals, and IT change templates.
Expand into regulated workflow libraries where auditability, portability, and cost control matter more than buying a single vendor suite.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The mechanism moves workflow ownership from one SaaS platform toward interoperable servers, portable definitions, and multi-party registries.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open BPMN tooling and workflow automation projects show credible primitives, but shared governance across enterprises is organizationally difficult.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The software primitives exist, but replacing ServiceNow's integrated admin, permissions, analytics, and enterprise support layer requires substantial engineering and governance.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could pressure pricing and portability, especially in public-sector and cooperative deployments, but is unlikely to displace large enterprise ServiceNow estates quickly.

products.name: IT Service Management
products.summary: ServiceNow IT Service Management unifies incident, problem, change, request, service catalog, and related IT operations workflows on the ServiceNow platform.
products.whyItMatters: ITSM is ServiceNow's historical beachhead and a key enterprise control point because it touches support tickets, change approvals, knowledge bases, assets, configuration data, and internal service delivery.
replacementSketch: A replacement can be much narrower than the full Now Platform: open-source ITSM, CMDB, help desk, asset inventory, and workflow tools can cover many core support and service-management needs.
For the largest organizations, the gap is usually less about ticketing and more about migration risk, process redesign, integrations, reporting, implementation partners, and executive confidence.

alternatives.name: GLPI
alternatives.summary: GLPI is an open-source IT service management and asset management platform covering service desk, inventory, incidents, and related IT operations workflows.

openness.rationale: GLPI is presented as an open-source ITSM and asset management solution with a public project and community ecosystem.

decentralizationFit.rationale: GLPI can be self-hosted and adapted locally, reducing SaaS dependence, though it is not a decentralized protocol by itself.

readiness.rationale: GLPI covers practical ITSM and asset-management needs, but enterprise ServiceNow migrations still require integration, process, and support work.

costLeverage.rationale: Open-source ITSM can materially reduce licensing pressure for organizations willing to operate their own service-management stack.

alternatives.name: iTop
alternatives.summary: iTop is an open-source ITSM and CMDB-oriented platform for service desk, incident, problem, change, and configuration management workflows.

openness.rationale: iTop is widely described as open-source ITSM software with CMDB-centered service management functionality.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Self-hosted CMDB and service-management control can reduce dependence on centralized SaaS, but iTop is still conventional application software.

readiness.rationale: iTop is plausible for CMDB-driven ITSM use cases, though very large enterprise deployments may still need substantial customization and integration.

costLeverage.rationale: An open-source CMDB-centered ITSM tool can reduce vendor lock-in and licensing leverage for teams with internal operations capacity.

disruptionConcepts.name: Cooperative ITSM Clearinghouse
disruptionConcepts.summary: A cooperative ITSM clearinghouse would let municipalities, universities, hospitals, nonprofits, and small enterprises share hardened ticket workflows, CMDB schemas, service catalogs, runbooks, and migration tooling around open-source ITSM systems.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: ServiceNow's advantage in repeatable enterprise service-management patterns weakens if peer institutions can jointly maintain and certify open ITSM playbooks instead of each buying the same centralized SaaS implementation.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is cooperative production and shared governance: members fund, review, and reuse common workflow assets while retaining local control of data and deployment.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Members pool funding for maintainers, vote on shared roadmap priorities, publish certified workflow packs, and use implementation partners that commit improvements back to the commons.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Workflow packs are tested against reference GLPI and iTop deployments, reviewed by member operators, signed by maintainers, and versioned with migration notes. Production incidents and security issues feed back into shared advisories.
failureModes: Shared workflows may fail to cover highly customized enterprise processes or regulated edge cases.
Cooperative governance can underfund maintenance if members consume shared assets without contributing back.
adoptionPath: Begin with shared service catalog, incident, request, asset, and change-management templates for similar public-sector or nonprofit organizations.
Add pooled migration tools, benchmark data, and certified hosting partners once the shared templates prove cheaper and easier to audit than bespoke SaaS implementations.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model distributes ownership of service-management process assets across member institutions instead of concentrating them in a single SaaS vendor.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open ITSM tools provide credible shared software foundations, but the cooperative maintenance and certification layer would need disciplined governance.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The concept can start with templates and migration tooling before attempting full ServiceNow parity, making phased implementation plausible.

incumbentPressure.rationale: A cooperative open ITSM stack can pressure smaller and mission-driven deployments, but ServiceNow's largest enterprise accounts retain deep integration and switching-cost advantages.

summaryMarkdown: Refreshed ServiceNow with Now Platform and ITSM coverage, current market metrics, open ITSM/workflow alternatives, and one disruption concept per product focused on federated workflow assets and cooperative ITSM operations.
